summary:
Pearl millet (Pennisetum glaucum) was domesticated from its wild ancestor Pennisetum violaceum in the West African Sahel, with the domestication process most likely occurring in the area of what is now southern Mauritania, Senegal, Mali, and Niger during the period approximately 4,500–3,500 BCE according to most current evidence — considerably later than the period of this era. However, the intensive use and harvesting of wild pearl millet in the Sahel region almost certainly predates full domestication, and the [[event:17|African Humid Period]] conditions of 10,000–8,000 BCE created the grassland and savanna environments in which the wild ancestors of pearl millet thrived across the West African interior.

During the early Holocene Green Sahara period, the distribution of wild Pennisetum grasses would have been considerably broader than today, extending across the now-hyperarid central Sahara, and communities in the West African interior may have been intensively exploiting these grasses millennia before formal domestication. Pearl millet's eventual domestication made it the most important staple grain of the West African Sahel and eventually a major crop across tropical Africa, India, and parts of Asia. Its drought tolerance and ability to grow in poor soils made it indispensable to communities in regions too dry or infertile for other cereals.
